headline:
The deal is signed.
The text is not public.
deck: The US-Iran MOU is being treated as politically operative before its legal terms are public. That gap lets Washington, Tehran, Israel, markets and Gulf intermediaries each act on a different version of the same promise.
body: Two days before the formal Switzerland ceremony, the central fact about the US-Iran peace MOU is still negative: neither the White House nor Iran has released the official text, while people shown the document told AP that circulating Al-Arabiya and Bloomberg versions broadly matched what they had seen. That leaves the public record stuck between claimed text, unofficial text and withheld text. [E1]

The absence is not only public-facing. Reuters reported that the pact has not been released or sent to Congress, and Senate Majority Leader John Thune said Republicans are trying to obtain it. The result is a political instrument that can be described as signed while remaining unavailable to the legislature that would normally scrutinize war powers, sanctions relief, nuclear concessions and any durable regional commitments. [E2]

Vice President JD Vance’s own description sharpened the ambiguity rather than closing it. He called the MOU “a very general document” of about “a page and half,” with details left to technical negotiations, while The Guardian reported that the formal ceremony is set for Friday 19 June at the Bürgenstock resort near Lucerne, Switzerland, confirmed by the Swiss foreign ministry. A short framework can de-escalate a crisis, but it cannot by itself settle the unresolved legal collisions it is now being asked to carry. [E3]

Those collisions are already visible. Reuters reported that how the deal will work “remains unclear”: Iran insists Lebanon must be covered, Israel says it is not bound and will not withdraw, the nuclear file is deferred to a 60-day negotiation, and the accord could generate a roughly $300bn reconstruction fund financed by Gulf states if Iran complies. Those are not drafting details. They are incompatible operating theories waiting for a text to expose them. [E4]

The document’s absence has not stopped operational behavior from changing. Reuters reported that at least three Iranian oil tankers carrying about 5 million barrels passed through the US blockade after the framework was reached, according to vessel-tracking data. That movement shows markets and governments already acting as if the MOU has force, even while its published legal basis remains absent. [E5]

The G7 closing position added a second layer of political validation. Leaders welcomed the US-Iran deal, demanded an immediate ceasefire in Lebanon, and reasserted Hormuz transit “without restrictions or tolls.” That language turns a non-public bilateral document into allied policy scaffolding, but it also puts pressure on the missing text to reconcile Iran’s Lebanon claim, Israel’s freedom of action and the maritime toll question in one enforceable formula. [E6]

The counter-read is that the secrecy is ordinary pre-ceremony choreography for a short framework, not proof of deception. On that view, the text is being held until the Swiss signing because it is politically fragile, and the tankers already moving through the blockade show the deal has real operational force regardless of whether Congress or the public has the paper in hand. The stronger version of that argument is procedural: a page-and-a-half MOU was never supposed to settle every technical question before the ceremony. [E3][E5]

The test on Friday is therefore not whether there is a ceremony. It is whether the official text, once published, can still support all the claims now being made in its name: toll-free Hormuz transit, Iranian face-saving management language, Israeli non-participation, a Lebanon ceasefire demand, a deferred nuclear negotiation and a Gulf-funded reconstruction bargain. Until then, “signed” is doing work that the text itself has not yet been allowed to do. [E2][E4][E6]

evidence_box.fragment: Neither the White House nor Iran has released the text of the agreement; people shown the MOU said the Al-Arabiya and Bloomberg versions broadly matched, but no official terms have been published.
The text of the pact has not been released or sent to Congress; Senate Majority Leader John Thune said Republicans are trying to get it.

JD Vance called the MOU “a very general document,” about “a page and half,” with details left to technical negotiations; the formal ceremony is set for Friday 19 June at the Bürgenstock resort near Lucerne, Switzerland, confirmed by the Swiss foreign ministry.

How the deal will work “remains unclear”: Iran insists Lebanon must be covered while Israel says it is not bound and will not withdraw; the nuclear program is deferred to a 60-day negotiation; the accord could create a roughly $300bn reconstruction fund financed by Gulf states if Iran complies.
At least three Iranian oil tankers carrying about 5 million barrels passed through the US blockade after the framework was reached, vessel-tracking data shows.
G7 leaders welcomed the US-Iran deal and demanded an immediate ceasefire in Lebanon, while reasserting Hormuz transit “without restrictions or tolls.”

art.title: Lebanon · the unresolved clause
art.caption: The spoiler the missing text leaves open: Iran says Lebanon is covered, Israel says it is not bound, the G7 demands a ceasefire · Map: Sprockett, Escalation Desk · Terrain: NOAA ETOPO1
